| Item | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Vadodara-Suncity, Gujarat (2.5 lakh sq ft land, 1.5 lakh sq ft built-up) | [tndindia](https://www.tndindia.com/trial-production-at-yash-highvoltage-transformer-bushing-factory-expected-in-h1fy27/) |
| Investment | **₹153 Cr** (capex ₹59.2 Cr already spent on PP&E in FY26) | [multibagg](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883), [scanx](https://scanx.trade/stock-market-news/companies/yash-highvoltage-advances-expansion-targets-trial-production-in-q2-fy-2026-27/41325144) |
| Product | RIP/RIS bushings **up to 550 kV** (vs current design capability to 220–245 kV) | scanx, multibagg |
| Capacity add | **~6,000 units/year** of RIP/RIS (vs current ~3,000 RIP units/yr at Halol) | [tndindia](https://www.tndindia.com/trial-production-at-yash-highvoltage-transformer-bushing-factory-expected-in-h1fy27/) |
| Trial production | **End Q2 FY27 (by ~Sep 2026)** | scanx, multibagg, tndindia |
| Commercial production | **H2 FY27 (~October 2026 onward)** | multibagg, constructionworld |
| Full capacity | ~3 years post-commissioning | tradebrains |

**Revenue potential:** The plate roughly doubles RIP capacity, and management ties the plant to the ₹550–700 Cr full-capacity revenue ambition. The plant is the vehicle for both **import substitution** (India's 400/765 kV bushings are largely imported today) and **export expansion** — management notes export realizations run **30–40% higher than domestic**, so the new high-voltage capacity is margin-accretive if export uptake materializes. ([tradebrains 174%](https://tradebrains.in/transformer-stock-that-delivered-174-returns-in-last-6-months-to-add-to-your-watchlist/))

**Schedule status (as of the latest commentary):** Civil work and office building complete; plant & machinery procurement complete; installation/commissioning ongoing, targeted to finish around end of Q1 FY27. ([scanx](https://scanx.trade/stock-market-news/companies/yash-highvoltage-advances-expansion-targets-trial-production-in-q2-fy-2026-27/41325144))

**Risks to the schedule:**
- **Type-testing / customer qualification lag** — even after commercial production starts, 400–550 kV bushings require lengthy type tests and OEM/utility homologation; revenue ramp typically trails commissioning by quarters. This is the single biggest gap between "commercial production H2 FY27" and "revenue."
- **In-house RIP-core dependency** — management is still developing in-house RIP core production to cut import reliance; the CMD acknowledges domestic supply is "concentrated." Core supply is a real bottleneck. ([tndindia interview, 25 Dec 2025](https://www.tndindia.com/we-see-strong-demand-for-rip-transformer-bushings-yash-highvoltage/))
- **Funding** — board approved a fund-raise of up to ₹150 Cr (≈₹100–110 Cr discussed); execution and dilution terms are not yet finalized. ([multibagg](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883))

> Note a minor source inconsistency: trial production is variously described as "H1 FY27" (by Sep 2026) and "end Q2 FY27." These are consistent (Q2 = the back end of H1). Commercial production is consistently H2 FY27.

---

## 4. International expansion

- **Yash HV USA Inc.** — incorporated in FY24-25 and **operationalized in FY26**; a full-time US-based BD manager hired to build the Americas footprint. The 550 kV plant is explicitly engineered to international (Americas/Europe) specs. ([multibagg](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883), [tradebrains](https://tradebrains.in/yash-highvoltages-post-listing-surge-signals-a-high-voltage-growth-story/))
- **Weidmann (Switzerland) agency & distribution deal** — a **48-month** agency agreement **effective 1 January 2025**. Weidmann Electrical Technology AG represents Yash for **OIP bushings up to 245 kV and high-current bushings up to 25 kA** across **Germany, Spain, France, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg, Portugal, Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia** (Europe + North Africa). Strategic logic: Yash gets channel access during a high-demand/long-lead-time period; Yash already has 35,000+ bushings installed across 60 countries. ([Transformer Magazine](https://transformer-magazine.com/tm-news/weidmann-and-yash-announce-strategic-partnership-in-european-and-north-african-markets/), [yashhv.com](https://www.yashhv.com/post/yash-and-weidmann-announce-strategic-partnership-in-european-and-north-african-markets))
- **UK/Ireland** — distribution via Electrolink. ([multibagg](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883))

**How material could exports become?** Exports were ~7% of FY26 revenue but have grown at a ~52.8% CAGR over 3 years ([goodreturns directors' report](https://www.goodreturns.in/company/yash-highvoltage/director-report.html)). Management targets **20%+ of revenue within 2–3 years**. Given export realizations are 30–40% higher than domestic, hitting that mix would be disproportionately margin-positive. The realism of 20% hinges almost entirely on the 550 kV plant qualifying for export markets on schedule — so Sections 3 and 4 are tightly coupled. Confidence on the 20% target: medium-low (it's a stated ambition, dependent on plant ramp and overseas qualification).

---

## 5. Sukrut Electric — 50% JV with Quality Power

- **Structure:** YHV acquired **50% of Sukrut Electric Company Pvt Ltd for ₹5.24 Cr**; the other 50% held by **Quality Power Electrical Equipments**. Binding term sheet signed **9 July 2025**; deal **completed ~9 January 2026** (acquired from a German multinational seller). ([scanx](https://scanx.trade/stock-market-news/orders-deals/yash-highvoltage-limited-completes-5-24-crore-acquisition-of-50-stake-in-sukrut-electric-company/29589926), [tndindia](https://www.tndindia.com/quality-power-and-yash-highvoltage-to-jointly-acquire-sukrut-electric/))
- **What Sukrut does:** Pune-based (est. 1967) maker of **transformer components**, supplying hundreds of Indian and global transformer OEMs.
- **Strategic rationale:** **Backward integration** into the transformer-component ecosystem — complementary product lines, new customer/technology access, and supply-chain security at a time when component supply is a sector bottleneck. CMD Keyur Shah lists it alongside the greenfield plant and R&D as a market-share lever. ([tndindia interview](https://www.tndindia.com/we-see-strong-demand-for-rip-transformer-bushings-yash-highvoltage/))
- **Contribution:** Sukrut FY26 revenue ~₹25–26 Cr, with a management **aspiration to ₹150–160 Cr over 4–5 years**. At 50% and ₹5.24 Cr cost, it's small near-term but a cheap optionality/strategic toehold. ([multibagg](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883))

---

## 6. Near-term catalysts (6–12 months) and de-rating triggers

**Upside catalysts:**
1. **550 kV plant milestones** — trial production by ~Q2 FY27 (Sep 2026) and commercial production H2 FY27 are the headline near-term proof-points.
2. **Order-inflow momentum** — progress toward the ₹500+ Cr FY27 booking target; any large 400/765 kV import-substitution wins.
3. **Export traction** — first material orders/realizations through the US subsidiary and Weidmann channel.
4. **Fund-raise clarity** — terms of the up-to-₹150 Cr raise (de-risks plant funding, but watch dilution).
5. **Sector tailwind** — strong global transformer demand from data centers, grid/renewables, EV and railway electrification (CMD's stated demand drivers, Dec 2025).
6. **Possible NSE migration / index inclusion** as the company scales beyond SME size (general SME-graduation catalyst; not yet confirmed).

**De-rating triggers / what could go wrong:**
1. **Plant slippage** — installation, type-testing, or qualification delays pushing commercial revenue beyond FY27; this is the highest-probability disappointment vector.
2. **Valuation/expectations risk** — the stock is up ~122% over 1 year and trades at **~54x trailing P/E** (₹735, ~₹2,102 Cr m-cap, P/B 11.4x). Much of the 5-year growth is already in the price; any execution wobble de-rates hard. ([screener](https://www.screener.in/company/544310/))
3. **RIP-core / raw-material supply** — concentrated domestic supply and import dependence for cores.
4. **Margin pressure** — if export mix or pricing disappoints, the 24–25% EBITDA guide is at risk; margins already dipped at the PAT line in H1 vs H2.
5. **Cyber incident** — a reported cyber incident in FY26 (loss expensed, investigation ongoing) — minor financially but a governance/controls flag to monitor. ([multibagg](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883))
6. **Equity dilution** from the fund-raise.
7. **SME liquidity** — thin float, low institutional ownership (FII 0.05%, DII 11%), high volatility.

---

## 7. Consensus / analyst forward estimates — FY27/FY28

**There is effectively no published sell-side consensus.** I checked Trendlyne and Investing.com (the usual consensus aggregators for Indian equities) and **neither displays FY27/FY28 revenue, EPS, or target-price estimates** for YASHHV — Trendlyne shows "share price target of [blank] for Unknown Year." Screener likewise shows no forward estimates. ([Trendlyne](https://trendlyne.com/equity/consensus-estimates/2845096/13140063/yash-highvoltage-ltd/), [Screener](https://www.screener.in/company/544310/))

This is normal for a BSE-SME microcap — formal brokerage coverage hasn't initiated. The only forward numbers in circulation are **management's own**: ~₹360–400 Cr FY27 revenue, 24–25% EBITDA margin, and the ₹550–700 Cr full-capacity / 3–4x-in-5-years ambition. Anyone modeling FY27/FY28 EPS today is extrapolating from management guidance, not from independent analyst estimates.

---

## Bottom line on the forward setup

**Upside drivers:** A doubling of RIP capacity plus a brand-new 550 kV high-voltage line (commercial H2 FY27) positioned squarely at import substitution and higher-margin exports; an order book that has 2.7x'd to ₹400 Cr giving 12–24 months visibility; a structural sector tailwind (grid/data-center/renewables transformer demand); and credible international channels (Weidmann 48-month deal, US subsidiary). Management guides ~40–45% FY27 growth at ~25% EBITDA margins and a 3–4x revenue ambition over 5 years.

**Downside risks:** The whole thesis leans on one plant ramping on schedule and qualifying for export markets — type-testing and RIP-core supply are the realistic slip points. The stock already discounts a lot (~54x P/E, +122% in a year), so execution misses de-rate sharply. Add SME-specific risks (liquidity, possible dilution from the ₹150 Cr raise, a flagged cyber incident, near-zero institutional coverage).

**Evidence quality:** FY26 actuals and the plant/JV/Weidmann structural facts are well-corroborated across multiple sources. The forward numbers (FY27 guide, ₹550–700 Cr potential, 20% export target) rest mostly on management commentary relayed through a small number of outlets (multibagg's concall summary, T&D India interviews, ScanX, TradeBrains) — directionally consistent but not independently modeled, and the headline ₹400-Cr 5-year piece on TradeBrains was inaccessible to me directly (403). No formal analyst consensus exists to cross-check against.

---

### Sources
- [multibagg.ai — YHV FY26 results / concall summary](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883)
- [ConstructionWorld — YHV Posts Record FY26 Consolidated Results](https://www.constructionworld.in/policy-updates-and-economic-news/yash-highvoltage-posts-record-fy26-consolidated-results/91679)
- [ScanX — Advances expansion, targets trial production Q2 FY27](https://scanx.trade/stock-market-news/companies/yash-highvoltage-advances-expansion-targets-trial-production-in-q2-fy-2026-27/41325144)
- [T&D India — Trial production expected H1FY27](https://www.tndindia.com/trial-production-at-yash-highvoltage-transformer-bushing-factory-expected-in-h1fy27/)
- [T&D India — "We see strong demand for RIP bushings" interview, 25 Dec 2025](https://www.tndindia.com/we-see-strong-demand-for-rip-transformer-bushings-yash-highvoltage/)
- [T&D India — Quality Power & Yash to jointly acquire Sukrut Electric](https://www.tndindia.com/quality-power-and-yash-highvoltage-to-jointly-acquire-sukrut-electric/)
- [ScanX — YHV completes ₹5.24 Cr 50% Sukrut acquisition](https://scanx.trade/stock-market-news/orders-deals/yash-highvoltage-limited-completes-5-24-crore-acquisition-of-50-stake-in-sukrut-electric-company/29589926)
- [Transformer Magazine — Weidmann & Yash strategic partnership](https://transformer-magazine.com/tm-news/weidmann-and-yash-announce-strategic-partnership-in-european-and-north-african-markets/)
- [Yash Highvoltage — Weidmann partnership announcement](https://www.yashhv.com/post/yash-and-weidmann-announce-strategic-partnership-in-european-and-north-african-markets)
- [TradeBrains — Transformer stock eyes 300–400% revenue surge over 5 years](https://tradebrains.in/high-growth-alert-transformer-stock-eyes-300-400-revenue-surge-over-the-next-5-years/) (403-blocked to me; figures via search summary only)
- [TradeBrains — 174% returns / revenue potential ₹550–700 Cr](https://tradebrains.in/transformer-stock-that-delivered-174-returns-in-last-6-months-to-add-to-your-watchlist/)
- [TradeBrains — Post-listing surge / exports CAGR & US subsidiary](https://tradebrains.in/yash-highvoltages-post-listing-surge-signals-a-high-voltage-growth-story/)
- [Goodreturns — YHV Directors' Report (exports 52.8% CAGR, USA Inc.)](https://www.goodreturns.in/company/yash-highvoltage/director-report.html)
- [Screener — YHV valuation & financials](https://www.screener.in/company/544310/)
- [Trendlyne — YHV consensus estimates (no estimates published)](https://trendlyne.com/equity/consensus-estimates/2845096/13140063/yash-highvoltage-ltd/)

aiTitle: Equity research on Yash High Voltage

content.text:
I now have enough corroborated data to write a thorough synthesis. Let me reconcile one important point: the FY26 actual balance sheet (consolidated) shows inventory ₹66.65 Cr and receivables ₹30.57 Cr, which conflicts with Screener's standalone "inventory" of ₹156 Cr. The Screener figure is clearly an aggregate "Other Assets" bucket and its derived "inventory days 194 / CCC 182" are unreliable. I have enough to present this candidly with appropriate caveats.

---

# Yash Highvoltage Ltd (BSE SME: 544310 / YASHHV) — Earnings Quality & Working Capital Forensic Review

**Question framed:** Is the gap between reported profit and operating cash flow at Yash Highvoltage a benign growth artifact, or a sign of weaker earnings quality? FY = year ending 31 March. Figures are as of FY26 full-year results (reported ~May 2026) unless noted.

**Important data caveat up front:** Yash reports both standalone and consolidated numbers, and the public aggregators are inconsistent about which they show and how they bucket balance-sheet lines. Screener's "inventory" line (₹154–156 Cr in FY25/FY26) is an **aggregated "Other Assets" bucket, not pure inventory** — the company's own FY26 disclosures put actual inventory at ₹66.65 Cr and receivables at ₹30.57 Cr. I therefore treat Screener's derived "inventory days 194 / CCC 182" as **unreliable** and flag it below. The CFO and PAT figures, by contrast, are consistent across Screener, Tickertape and the company's own cash-flow statement, so I have high confidence in them.

---

## 1. CFO vs PAT, FY22–FY26, and CFO/PAT ratio

| FY | PAT (₹ Cr) | CFO (₹ Cr) | CFO/PAT |
|----|-----------|-----------|---------|
| FY22 | 8.72 | 7.28 | **0.83** |
| FY23 | 11.43 | 9.12 | **0.80** |
| FY24 | 12.31 | 14.41 | **1.17** |
| FY25 | 21.40 | 9.19 | **0.43** |
| FY26 | 37.34 | 8.82 | **0.24** |

Sources: PAT and CFO FY22–FY25 from Screener.in and Tickertape (which agree to two decimals); FY26 CFO of ₹882.02 Lakhs (₹8.82 Cr) and FY26 PAT ₹37.34 Cr from the company's FY26 results / annual disclosure as reported by ScanX/Trendlyne (May 2026).

**Read:** The aggregator flag is real and, if anything, understated. Cumulative FY22–FY26 PAT is ~₹91 Cr against cumulative CFO of ~₹49 Cr — roughly **54 cents of operating cash per rupee of book profit over five years**, and the conversion is *deteriorating sharply* as the company scales: 1.17 in FY24 → 0.43 in FY25 → **~0.24 in FY26**. An independent analyst write-up (anigup.com) had already flagged FY25 at 0.43 as "profits rise, but cash doesn't" and set an explicit "kill-switch" of CFO/PAT < 0.6 for multiple quarters; FY26 breaches that further. This is the single most important finding: **profit growth is accelerating while operating cash conversion is collapsing.**

---

## 2. Working capital cycle, FY22–FY26

Two competing data sets exist. I present both and tell you which to trust.

**(a) Screener-derived days** (use with caution — inventory line is an aggregated bucket):

| Metric (days) | FY22 | FY23 | FY24 | FY25 | FY26 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inventory days | 130 | 126 | 143 | 138 | 194 |
| Debtor days | 53 | 66 | 42 | 68 | 47 |
| Payable days | 81 | 110 | 103 | 93 | 60 |
| Cash conversion cycle | 102 | 82 | 82 | 112 | 182 |

**(b) Actual FY26 vs FY25 balance-sheet levels** (from company disclosures):
- Inventory: ₹29.67 Cr (FY25) → **₹66.65 Cr (FY26)**, +125%
- Trade receivables: ₹27.73 Cr (FY25) → **₹30.57 Cr (FY26)**, +10%
- Revenue: ₹149.6 Cr → ₹235.2 Cr, +57%

**Read:** The two pictures partly conflict, and the reconciliation is the crux of the story.
- On **actual levels**, receivables are *well-behaved* — they grew only ~10% against 57% revenue growth, so debtor days actually *fell* (consistent with Screener's FY26 debtor days dropping to 47). That contradicts the "receivables rising faster than sales" worry for FY26 specifically; that concern was more a FY23/FY25 phenomenon (debtor days spiked to 66 and 68 in those years).
- **Inventory is the genuine pressure point.** Actual inventory more than doubled (₹30 Cr → ₹67 Cr) — far outpacing the 57% revenue rise — which is what drags the CCC out and starves CFO. Screener's CCC blowing out to 182 days is directionally right (inventory-driven) even if the absolute "194 inventory days" overstates it because of the bucket-aggregation issue.
- **Payable days are shrinking** (81→60 over the period; FY26 ~60), meaning Yash is *paying suppliers faster* even as it holds more inventory — a double squeeze on cash. The combination of (rising inventory + faster supplier payment) is the mechanical reason CFO is so far below PAT.

So the working-capital cycle is **lengthening, and it is inventory- and payables-driven, not receivables-driven**, in the most recent year.

---

## 3. Growth-financing or deeper quality problem?

My read: **predominantly growth-and-scaling, with two legitimate yellow flags that prevent a fully clean bill of health.**

Arguments that it is benign growth financing:
- Revenue +57%, unit volumes from 5,752 → 7,272 (+26%) in FY26 — real physical output, not paper sales. Demand is corroborated by an **order book of ₹400+ Cr** (up from ~₹150 Cr a year earlier), so inventory is being built against visible backlog, not stuffed against weak demand.
- Receivables grew *slower* than sales in FY26 — the opposite of channel-stuffing behaviour.
- The business is genuinely working-capital-intensive (long-lead high-voltage bushings; long inventory cycle is structural — inventory days have sat at 126–143 for years).

Yellow flags that keep this from being fully clean:
- A **five-year average CFO/PAT near 0.5** is low even for a working-capital-heavy capital-goods firm; healthy peers usually convert more over a full cycle. The cash gap is persistent, not a one-year blip.
- The independent analyst write-up specifically flags **"other current assets/advances jump with no clarity"** as an opacity concern. This is exactly the line item that the aggregators are mis-bucketing as "inventory," and it is where I have the **least visibility** — it deserves direct scrutiny in the annual report notes.

Net: I judge this **~70% growth-funding, ~30% quality-flag.** The inventory build is plausibly demand-backed, but the *magnitude* of the cash shortfall and the opacity of the "other assets" bucket mean it cannot be waved through as purely benign. Confidence: moderate, limited by my inability to see the audited note-level breakdown of current assets.

---

## 4. Capex and how the expansion is funded

| FY | Capex (₹ Cr, PP&E additions) |
|----|------|
| FY22 | ~14 |
| FY23 | ~17 |
| FY24 | ~18 |
| FY25 | ~41 |
| FY26 | **59.2** (₹5,920.15 Lakhs, per FY26 cash-flow statement) |

- The flagship project is a **greenfield RIP/RIS condenser-graded bushing plant in Vadodara, Gujarat**, total outlay **~₹153 Cr**, capacity +6,000 units/yr, scope upgraded from 220 kV to **550 kV range**. Trial production targeted Q2 FY27, commercial production H2 FY27. Capital WIP rose from ₹2.39 Cr (FY25) to **₹42.50 Cr (FY26)**, confirming the build-out is mid-flight.
- **Funding stack:**
  1. **IPO proceeds** — December 2024 BSE SME IPO of ₹110 Cr (75.35 lakh shares at face value ₹5; subscribed ~169x). Of net proceeds, **₹70.34 Cr was explicitly earmarked for the new RIP/RIS plant**, balance for general corporate purposes. Company/ScanX confirm the 550 kV facility is being funded "using IPO proceeds."
  2. **Internal accruals / deposits** — bank balances other than cash fell from ₹46.69 Cr (Mar-25) to nil (Mar-26), and cash fell ₹24.91 Cr → ₹20.06 Cr; fixed deposits were redeployed into capex and working capital.
  3. **A further equity fundraise** — board approved (13 May 2026 meeting) raising **up to ₹150 Cr** (management indicated ~₹100–110 Cr would actually be raised) primarily for the capex and testing infrastructure. So the ₹153 Cr project is **IPO money + internal accruals now, topped up by a fresh equity raise** — not predominantly debt.
- **Leverage stays conservative:** borrowings rose ₹23 Cr (FY25) → ₹38 Cr (FY26), but **debt-to-equity is only 0.17x**. The company is funding growth largely with equity, which protects the balance sheet but (see §5) dilutes return ratios.

---

## 5. Return ratios and post-IPO sustainability

| FY | ROE | ROCE |
|----|-----|------|
| FY22 | 48% | 52% |
| FY23 | 45% | 45% |
| FY24 | 28% | 28% |
| FY25 | 29% | 29% |

Sources: Screener (FY26 ROE/ROCE not yet cleanly published at time of review).

**Read:** Returns have **already stepped down sharply** — from ~50% to ~28–29% — and this is a *direct consequence of the equity-funded model*. The IPO (Dec 2024) added ~₹110 Cr and a fresh ₹100–150 Cr raise is coming, which inflates the denominator (equity/capital employed) faster than profit can fill it. Reserves jumped from ₹39 Cr (FY24) to ₹133 Cr (FY25) to ₹170 Cr (FY26) — equity base more than 4x in two years.

Why the high-20s% may not hold in the near term:
- The ~₹153 Cr greenfield plant is **not yet generating revenue** (commercial production only H2 FY27), so it currently sits in capital employed as a drag with zero return — mechanically depressing ROCE until utilisation ramps.
- A second equity raise further dilutes ROE before the new capacity earns.

So expect ROCE/ROE to **compress further (potentially into the low-20s or high-teens) in FY27 as capital lands ahead of earnings**, then recover *if* the 550 kV capacity utilises well. The high return ratios of FY22–23 reflected a tiny, lightly-capitalised base and are **not a sustainable baseline**. Whether the new, larger equity base can earn a high-20s ROCE is the open question — it depends entirely on plant utilisation and pricing, which won't be visible until FY27–FY28.

---

## 6. Aggressive revenue recognition / related-party / inventory red flags

- **Revenue recognition:** No hard evidence of aggressive recognition. Volume growth (5,752 → 7,272 units) tracks revenue growth, receivables grew *slower* than sales in FY26, and there is a corroborated ₹400+ Cr order book. These are the *opposite* of channel-stuffing signatures. **No red flag on recognition for FY26**, though FY23 and FY25 debtor-day spikes (66 and 68 days) warrant a look at whether revenue was being pulled forward in those specific years.
- **Related-party transactions:** The **Sukrut Electric** 50% stake acquisition (₹5.24 Cr, JV with Quality Power Electrical Equipments) was **explicitly disclosed as NOT a related-party transaction** — promoter/promoter group has no prior interest in Sukrut. The **Yash HV USA Inc** subsidiary and European/UK distribution build-out are organic expansion. I found **no disclosed promoter-conflicted RPTs** in the public record. Caveat: I did not have access to the audited related-party note in the annual report, so this is "nothing surfaced," not "verified clean."
- **Inventory build vs demand:** Inventory more than doubled (₹30 → ₹67 Cr). This is the **one item that doesn't fully self-explain.** Part is plausibly pre-stocking for the order book and the new plant ramp, but the build outpaces even the 57% revenue growth and is the primary cause of weak CFO. Combined with the analyst-flagged opacity of the "other current assets/advances" line, **inventory is the item to interrogate directly in the FY26 annual report notes.** I cannot, from public sources, confirm it is fully demand-matched.

---

## Synthesis — candid read on earnings quality

- **The cash-flow flag is genuine and worsening.** CFO/PAT fell from 1.17 (FY24) to 0.43 (FY25) to ~0.24 (FY26); five-year cash conversion is roughly half of reported profit. This is the headline earnings-quality issue and it is not a one-off.
- **The cause is mechanical and mostly explainable: an inventory build (₹30→₹67 Cr) plus shrinking payable days, in a structurally working-capital-heavy business that is scaling 57%/yr against a real, growing order book.** Receivables — the classic manipulation vector — are actually *well-behaved* in FY26.
- **It is therefore more "growth eating cash" than "phantom profits,"** but two reservations stop me short of a clean verdict: (1) the persistent five-year cash shortfall is large for the sector, and (2) the "other current assets/advances" bucket is opaque in the public data and is exactly where aggregators are mis-classifying balances. Earnings quality is **"acceptable but on watch"**, not pristine.
- **Returns are de-rating by design** as the equity base balloons post-IPO (and a further ₹100–150 Cr raise is coming); the ~50% ROCE of FY22–23 is gone and high-20s% is itself at risk in FY27 until the ₹153 Cr 550 kV plant utilises.
- **No surfaced evidence of fraud, aggressive recognition, or promoter-conflicted RPTs.** Sukrut is confirmed non-related-party.

**What I could not verify (thin evidence):** note-level breakdown of "other current assets/advances"; the audited related-party schedule; FY26 ROCE/ROE (not yet cleanly published); and the precise consolidated-vs-standalone basis behind some aggregator day-metrics. The three items most worth pulling from the **FY26 audited annual report** are (1) the current-assets/advances note, (2) the inventory ageing/composition, and (3) the related-party schedule. As a fast-moving SME with a major capex mid-flight, figures here are current only to ~May–June 2026.

---

### Sources

- [Screener.in — Yash Highvoltage Ltd (544310)](https://www.screener.in/company/544310/) — CFO, PAT, days metrics, ROE/ROCE, borrowings, capex, reserves FY22–FY26
- [Tickertape — Yash Highvoltage (YASHH)](https://www.tickertape.in/stocks/yash-highvoltage-YASHH) — CFO, PAT, working-capital change, FCF FY22–FY25
- [anigup.com — Yash HighVoltage stock research](https://www.anigup.com/post/yash-highvoltage) — CFO/PAT ratio series, earnings-quality flags, kill-switch thresholds
- [Construction World — Yash Highvoltage FY26 consolidated results](https://www.constructionworld.in/policy-updates-and-economic-news/yash-highvoltage-posts-record-fy26-consolidated-results/91679) — FY26 revenue/EBITDA/PAT, volumes

toolUseResult.prompt:
Deep financial-quality analysis on "Yash Highvoltage Limited" (BSE SME 544310), an Indian high-voltage transformer-bushing maker. FY = year ending 31 March.

I want a forensic look at EARNINGS QUALITY and WORKING CAPITAL, because aggregators flag that cash flow from operations is lagging reported profit. Pull data from Screener.in, Tickertape, Trendlyne, and any analyst write-ups.

Investigate and report, with figures and sources:
1. Year-by-year cash flow from operations (CFO) vs net profit (PAT) for FY22–FY26. Compute CFO/PAT ratio each year.
2. Working capital cycle trend: inventory days, debtor/receivable days, payable days, and overall cash conversion cycle, FY22–FY26.
3. Is the deteriorating cash conversion a function of rapid growth (financing receivables/inventory to scale) or a deeper quality problem? Reason it through.
4. Capex trend and how the expansion (550 kV plant, ~₹59 Cr FY26 capex) is being funded — internal accruals, debt, or the IPO/fundraise.
5. Return ratios (ROCE, ROE) trend and whether they're sustainable as the equity base grows post-IPO.
6. Any signs of aggressive revenue recognition, related-party transactions, or inventory build-up that doesn't match demand.

Give a clear, well-cited synthesis with a candid read on earnings quality. Findings only, no buy/sell call. Cite sources and figure dates.

content.text:
I now have enough corroborated data to write a thorough synthesis. Let me reconcile one important point: the FY26 actual balance sheet (consolidated) shows inventory ₹66.65 Cr and receivables ₹30.57 Cr, which conflicts with Screener's standalone "inventory" of ₹156 Cr. The Screener figure is clearly an aggregate "Other Assets" bucket and its derived "inventory days 194 / CCC 182" are unreliable. I have enough to present this candidly with appropriate caveats.

---

# Yash Highvoltage Ltd (BSE SME: 544310 / YASHHV) — Earnings Quality & Working Capital Forensic Review

**Question framed:** Is the gap between reported profit and operating cash flow at Yash Highvoltage a benign growth artifact, or a sign of weaker earnings quality? FY = year ending 31 March. Figures are as of FY26 full-year results (reported ~May 2026) unless noted.

**Important data caveat up front:** Yash reports both standalone and consolidated numbers, and the public aggregators are inconsistent about which they show and how they bucket balance-sheet lines. Screener's "inventory" line (₹154–156 Cr in FY25/FY26) is an **aggregated "Other Assets" bucket, not pure inventory** — the company's own FY26 disclosures put actual inventory at ₹66.65 Cr and receivables at ₹30.57 Cr. I therefore treat Screener's derived "inventory days 194 / CCC 182" as **unreliable** and flag it below. The CFO and PAT figures, by contrast, are consistent across Screener, Tickertape and the company's own cash-flow statement, so I have high confidence in them.

---

## 1. CFO vs PAT, FY22–FY26, and CFO/PAT ratio

| FY | PAT (₹ Cr) | CFO (₹ Cr) | CFO/PAT |
|----|-----------|-----------|---------|
| FY22 | 8.72 | 7.28 | **0.83** |
| FY23 | 11.43 | 9.12 | **0.80** |
| FY24 | 12.31 | 14.41 | **1.17** |
| FY25 | 21.40 | 9.19 | **0.43** |
| FY26 | 37.34 | 8.82 | **0.24** |

Sources: PAT and CFO FY22–FY25 from Screener.in and Tickertape (which agree to two decimals); FY26 CFO of ₹882.02 Lakhs (₹8.82 Cr) and FY26 PAT ₹37.34 Cr from the company's FY26 results / annual disclosure as reported by ScanX/Trendlyne (May 2026).

**Read:** The aggregator flag is real and, if anything, understated. Cumulative FY22–FY26 PAT is ~₹91 Cr against cumulative CFO of ~₹49 Cr — roughly **54 cents of operating cash per rupee of book profit over five years**, and the conversion is *deteriorating sharply* as the company scales: 1.17 in FY24 → 0.43 in FY25 → **~0.24 in FY26**. An independent analyst write-up (anigup.com) had already flagged FY25 at 0.43 as "profits rise, but cash doesn't" and set an explicit "kill-switch" of CFO/PAT < 0.6 for multiple quarters; FY26 breaches that further. This is the single most important finding: **profit growth is accelerating while operating cash conversion is collapsing.**

---

## 2. Working capital cycle, FY22–FY26

Two competing data sets exist. I present both and tell you which to trust.

**(a) Screener-derived days** (use with caution — inventory line is an aggregated bucket):

| Metric (days) | FY22 | FY23 | FY24 | FY25 | FY26 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inventory days | 130 | 126 | 143 | 138 | 194 |
| Debtor days | 53 | 66 | 42 | 68 | 47 |
| Payable days | 81 | 110 | 103 | 93 | 60 |
| Cash conversion cycle | 102 | 82 | 82 | 112 | 182 |

**(b) Actual FY26 vs FY25 balance-sheet levels** (from company disclosures):
- Inventory: ₹29.67 Cr (FY25) → **₹66.65 Cr (FY26)**, +125%
- Trade receivables: ₹27.73 Cr (FY25) → **₹30.57 Cr (FY26)**, +10%
- Revenue: ₹149.6 Cr → ₹235.2 Cr, +57%

**Read:** The two pictures partly conflict, and the reconciliation is the crux of the story.
- On **actual levels**, receivables are *well-behaved* — they grew only ~10% against 57% revenue growth, so debtor days actually *fell* (consistent with Screener's FY26 debtor days dropping to 47). That contradicts the "receivables rising faster than sales" worry for FY26 specifically; that concern was more a FY23/FY25 phenomenon (debtor days spiked to 66 and 68 in those years).
- **Inventory is the genuine pressure point.** Actual inventory more than doubled (₹30 Cr → ₹67 Cr) — far outpacing the 57% revenue rise — which is what drags the CCC out and starves CFO. Screener's CCC blowing out to 182 days is directionally right (inventory-driven) even if the absolute "194 inventory days" overstates it because of the bucket-aggregation issue.
- **Payable days are shrinking** (81→60 over the period; FY26 ~60), meaning Yash is *paying suppliers faster* even as it holds more inventory — a double squeeze on cash. The combination of (rising inventory + faster supplier payment) is the mechanical reason CFO is so far below PAT.

So the working-capital cycle is **lengthening, and it is inventory- and payables-driven, not receivables-driven**, in the most recent year.

---

## 3. Growth-financing or deeper quality problem?

My read: **predominantly growth-and-scaling, with two legitimate yellow flags that prevent a fully clean bill of health.**

Arguments that it is benign growth financing:
- Revenue +57%, unit volumes from 5,752 → 7,272 (+26%) in FY26 — real physical output, not paper sales. Demand is corroborated by an **order book of ₹400+ Cr** (up from ~₹150 Cr a year earlier), so inventory is being built against visible backlog, not stuffed against weak demand.
- Receivables grew *slower* than sales in FY26 — the opposite of channel-stuffing behaviour.
- The business is genuinely working-capital-intensive (long-lead high-voltage bushings; long inventory cycle is structural — inventory days have sat at 126–143 for years).

Yellow flags that keep this from being fully clean:
- A **five-year average CFO/PAT near 0.5** is low even for a working-capital-heavy capital-goods firm; healthy peers usually convert more over a full cycle. The cash gap is persistent, not a one-year blip.
- The independent analyst write-up specifically flags **"other current assets/advances jump with no clarity"** as an opacity concern. This is exactly the line item that the aggregators are mis-bucketing as "inventory," and it is where I have the **least visibility** — it deserves direct scrutiny in the annual report notes.

Net: I judge this **~70% growth-funding, ~30% quality-flag.** The inventory build is plausibly demand-backed, but the *magnitude* of the cash shortfall and the opacity of the "other assets" bucket mean it cannot be waved through as purely benign. Confidence: moderate, limited by my inability to see the audited note-level breakdown of current assets.

---

## 4. Capex and how the expansion is funded

| FY | Capex (₹ Cr, PP&E additions) |
|----|------|
| FY22 | ~14 |
| FY23 | ~17 |
| FY24 | ~18 |
| FY25 | ~41 |
| FY26 | **59.2** (₹5,920.15 Lakhs, per FY26 cash-flow statement) |

- The flagship project is a **greenfield RIP/RIS condenser-graded bushing plant in Vadodara, Gujarat**, total outlay **~₹153 Cr**, capacity +6,000 units/yr, scope upgraded from 220 kV to **550 kV range**. Trial production targeted Q2 FY27, commercial production H2 FY27. Capital WIP rose from ₹2.39 Cr (FY25) to **₹42.50 Cr (FY26)**, confirming the build-out is mid-flight.
- **Funding stack:**
  1. **IPO proceeds** — December 2024 BSE SME IPO of ₹110 Cr (75.35 lakh shares at face value ₹5; subscribed ~169x). Of net proceeds, **₹70.34 Cr was explicitly earmarked for the new RIP/RIS plant**, balance for general corporate purposes. Company/ScanX confirm the 550 kV facility is being funded "using IPO proceeds."
  2. **Internal accruals / deposits** — bank balances other than cash fell from ₹46.69 Cr (Mar-25) to nil (Mar-26), and cash fell ₹24.91 Cr → ₹20.06 Cr; fixed deposits were redeployed into capex and working capital.
  3. **A further equity fundraise** — board approved (13 May 2026 meeting) raising **up to ₹150 Cr** (management indicated ~₹100–110 Cr would actually be raised) primarily for the capex and testing infrastructure. So the ₹153 Cr project is **IPO money + internal accruals now, topped up by a fresh equity raise** — not predominantly debt.
- **Leverage stays conservative:** borrowings rose ₹23 Cr (FY25) → ₹38 Cr (FY26), but **debt-to-equity is only 0.17x**. The company is funding growth largely with equity, which protects the balance sheet but (see §5) dilutes return ratios.

---

## 5. Return ratios and post-IPO sustainability

| FY | ROE | ROCE |
|----|-----|------|
| FY22 | 48% | 52% |
| FY23 | 45% | 45% |
| FY24 | 28% | 28% |
| FY25 | 29% | 29% |

Sources: Screener (FY26 ROE/ROCE not yet cleanly published at time of review).

**Read:** Returns have **already stepped down sharply** — from ~50% to ~28–29% — and this is a *direct consequence of the equity-funded model*. The IPO (Dec 2024) added ~₹110 Cr and a fresh ₹100–150 Cr raise is coming, which inflates the denominator (equity/capital employed) faster than profit can fill it. Reserves jumped from ₹39 Cr (FY24) to ₹133 Cr (FY25) to ₹170 Cr (FY26) — equity base more than 4x in two years.

Why the high-20s% may not hold in the near term:
- The ~₹153 Cr greenfield plant is **not yet generating revenue** (commercial production only H2 FY27), so it currently sits in capital employed as a drag with zero return — mechanically depressing ROCE until utilisation ramps.
- A second equity raise further dilutes ROE before the new capacity earns.

So expect ROCE/ROE to **compress further (potentially into the low-20s or high-teens) in FY27 as capital lands ahead of earnings**, then recover *if* the 550 kV capacity utilises well. The high return ratios of FY22–23 reflected a tiny, lightly-capitalised base and are **not a sustainable baseline**. Whether the new, larger equity base can earn a high-20s ROCE is the open question — it depends entirely on plant utilisation and pricing, which won't be visible until FY27–FY28.

---

## 6. Aggressive revenue recognition / related-party / inventory red flags

- **Revenue recognition:** No hard evidence of aggressive recognition. Volume growth (5,752 → 7,272 units) tracks revenue growth, receivables grew *slower* than sales in FY26, and there is a corroborated ₹400+ Cr order book. These are the *opposite* of channel-stuffing signatures. **No red flag on recognition for FY26**, though FY23 and FY25 debtor-day spikes (66 and 68 days) warrant a look at whether revenue was being pulled forward in those specific years.
- **Related-party transactions:** The **Sukrut Electric** 50% stake acquisition (₹5.24 Cr, JV with Quality Power Electrical Equipments) was **explicitly disclosed as NOT a related-party transaction** — promoter/promoter group has no prior interest in Sukrut. The **Yash HV USA Inc** subsidiary and European/UK distribution build-out are organic expansion. I found **no disclosed promoter-conflicted RPTs** in the public record. Caveat: I did not have access to the audited related-party note in the annual report, so this is "nothing surfaced," not "verified clean."
- **Inventory build vs demand:** Inventory more than doubled (₹30 → ₹67 Cr). This is the **one item that doesn't fully self-explain.** Part is plausibly pre-stocking for the order book and the new plant ramp, but the build outpaces even the 57% revenue growth and is the primary cause of weak CFO. Combined with the analyst-flagged opacity of the "other current assets/advances" line, **inventory is the item to interrogate directly in the FY26 annual report notes.** I cannot, from public sources, confirm it is fully demand-matched.

---

## Synthesis — candid read on earnings quality

- **The cash-flow flag is genuine and worsening.** CFO/PAT fell from 1.17 (FY24) to 0.43 (FY25) to ~0.24 (FY26); five-year cash conversion is roughly half of reported profit. This is the headline earnings-quality issue and it is not a one-off.
- **The cause is mechanical and mostly explainable: an inventory build (₹30→₹67 Cr) plus shrinking payable days, in a structurally working-capital-heavy business that is scaling 57%/yr against a real, growing order book.** Receivables — the classic manipulation vector — are actually *well-behaved* in FY26.
- **It is therefore more "growth eating cash" than "phantom profits,"** but two reservations stop me short of a clean verdict: (1) the persistent five-year cash shortfall is large for the sector, and (2) the "other current assets/advances" bucket is opaque in the public data and is exactly where aggregators are mis-classifying balances. Earnings quality is **"acceptable but on watch"**, not pristine.
- **Returns are de-rating by design** as the equity base balloons post-IPO (and a further ₹100–150 Cr raise is coming); the ~50% ROCE of FY22–23 is gone and high-20s% is itself at risk in FY27 until the ₹153 Cr 550 kV plant utilises.
- **No surfaced evidence of fraud, aggressive recognition, or promoter-conflicted RPTs.** Sukrut is confirmed non-related-party.

**What I could not verify (thin evidence):** note-level breakdown of "other current assets/advances"; the audited related-party schedule; FY26 ROCE/ROE (not yet cleanly published); and the precise consolidated-vs-standalone basis behind some aggregator day-metrics. The three items most worth pulling from the **FY26 audited annual report** are (1) the current-assets/advances note, (2) the inventory ageing/composition, and (3) the related-party schedule. As a fast-moving SME with a major capex mid-flight, figures here are current only to ~May–June 2026.

---

### Sources

- [Screener.in — Yash Highvoltage Ltd (544310)](https://www.screener.in/company/544310/) — CFO, PAT, days metrics, ROE/ROCE, borrowings, capex, reserves FY22–FY26
- [Tickertape — Yash Highvoltage (YASHH)](https://www.tickertape.in/stocks/yash-highvoltage-YASHH) — CFO, PAT, working-capital change, FCF FY22–FY25
- [anigup.com — Yash HighVoltage stock research](https://www.anigup.com/post/yash-highvoltage) — CFO/PAT ratio series, earnings-quality flags, kill-switch thresholds
- [Construction World — Yash Highvoltage FY26 consolidated results](https://www.constructionworld.in/policy-updates-and-economic-news/yash-highvoltage-posts-record-fy26-consolidated-results/91679) — FY26 revenue/EBITDA/PAT, volumes
- [Multibagg.ai — Yash FY26 results / order book](https://www.multibagg.ai/market-pulse/articles/yash-fy26-results-93883) — ₹153 Cr greenfield, ₹400 Cr order book, ₹100–150 Cr fundraise, D/E 0.17x, CWIP ₹42.5 Cr
- [ScanX — Yash advances expansion, trial production Q2 FY27](https://scanx.trade/stock-market-news/companies/yash-highvoltage-advances-expansion-targets-trial-production-in-q2-fy-2026-27/41325144) — FY26 CFO ₹882.02 L, capex ₹5,920.15 L, IPO-proceeds funding, total assets, D/E
- [ScanX — Sukrut Electric 50% acquisition (not a related party)](https://scanx.trade/stock-market-news/orders-deals/yash-highvoltage-limited-completes-5-24-crore-acquisition-of-50-stake-in-sukrut-electric-company/29589926)
- [Chittorgarh — Yash Highvoltage IPO details](https://www.chittorgarh.com/ipo/yash-highvoltage-ipo/1929/) and [Business Standard — IPO 169x subscribed](https://www.business-standard.com/markets/capital-market-news/bse-sme-yash-highvoltage-s-ipo-ends-with-169-times-subscription-124121700265_1.html) — Dec-2024 ₹110 Cr IPO, ₹70.34 Cr earmarked for RIP/RIS plant
- [Whalesbook — FY26 profit +75%, revenue +57%](https://www.whalesbook.com/corporate-news/English/industrial-goodsservices/Yash-Highvoltage-FY26-Profit-Surges-75percent-Revenue-Up-57percent/6a04f8659444064ed0a1f8b5) — ₹153 Cr investment / near-debt-free commentary

This is a findings-only forensic read with no investment recommendation, as requested.

aiTitle: Equity research on Yash High Voltage

content.text:
I now have enough to build a complete, well-cited synthesis. Here is my report.

---

# Yash Highvoltage Ltd (BSE SME 544310) — Valuation Read

**Framing:** This is a relative- and growth-adjusted valuation read on Yash Highvoltage, a ~₹2,100 Cr high-voltage transformer-bushing maker trading at ~54x P/E and ~11x P/B as of mid-June 2026. I assess where its multiple sits versus T&D-equipment peers, whether the premium is justified by growth/margins, what growth the multiple implies, the post-listing re-rating, available analyst views, and sensitivity to growth normalization. Findings only — no buy/sell.

**Important data caveats up front:**
- Yash is a **BSE SME** stock. Sell-side broker coverage is essentially absent — Trendlyne shows "No estimates available," and no mainstream brokerage price target exists. The only "targets" I found are from low-quality forecasting blogs (moneymintidea), which I do not treat as credible.
- Yash's own data has a **reported inconsistency**: Tickertape shows an FY24 EPS of ₹40.09 then FY25 EPS of ₹7.50 — that jump reflects a bonus/split or face-value change (FV is ₹5), not a real collapse. Treat per-share history pre-listing with caution; absolute revenue/profit figures are reliable.
- Figures are pulled across Screener, Tickertape, Trendlyne and Groww between ~9–13 June 2026; small differences across platforms reflect timing and TTM-vs-FY conventions. I flag these where they matter.

## Yash baseline (Screener/Tickertape, ~12 June 2026)
- Price ~₹735; market cap ~₹2,102 Cr; **P/E ~54** (Screener) to ~56 (Tickertape); **P/B ~11.4x** (Screener); EV/EBITDA ~30x (per ValueResearch/MarketsMojo data).
- FY26 sales ₹235 Cr, net profit ₹37 Cr, OPM ~24–25%, ROCE ~28.7%, ROE ~23.5%.
- Growth: FY24 ₹108 Cr → FY25 ₹150 Cr → FY26 ₹235 Cr revenue (~57% TTM sales growth, ~44% 5-yr CAGR). H1 FY26 revenue reportedly +78.6% YoY.
- Order book ~₹300–400 Cr; management guidance of ~35–40% CAGR over 5 years (and an aspirational "8–10x in 10 years"); Savli greenfield plant commercial production targeted H2 FY27.

## 1. Peer comparison table

All figures sourced from Screener.in company pages (consolidated where applicable), data dated ~2–12 June 2026. "Closest comparables" judgment follows the table.

| Company | Mkt cap (₹ Cr) | P/E | P/B | EV/EBITDA | Rev growth (TTM) | ROCE | OPM (FY26) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Yash Highvoltage** | ~2,102 | **~54** | **~11.4** | ~30 | ~57% | 28.7% | ~24% |
| Shilchar Technologies | ~4,616 | 29.2 | 9.4 | n/a | ~5% (3-yr 33%) | 50.7% | 29% |
| Voltamp Transformers | ~9,833 | 32.2 | ~5.5* | n/a | ~mid-teens | 24% | 16% |
| Transformers & Rectifiers (TARIL) | ~9,458 | 35.8 | 6.2 | n/a | 24% | 23.3% | 15% |
| Hitachi Energy India (POWERINDIA) | ~1,52,995 | 149 | 29.6 | n/a | 28% | 29.0% | 15% |
| GE Vernova T&D India (GVT&D) | ~1,26,553 | ~99 | 47.0 | n/a | 45% | 76.4% | 27% |
| CG Power & Industrial | ~1,44,022 | 117 | 18.1 | n/a | 25% | 27.0% | 13% |
| Apar Industries | ~61,149 | 61.1 | 11.3 | ~21 | 23% | 31.1% | 8% |
| HBL Engineering (ex-HBL Power) | ~21,463 | 26.2 | 9.9 | n/a | 67% | 59.4% | 34% |

\*Voltamp P/B computed from price ₹9,720 / book ₹1,771 ≈ 5.5x. EV/EBITDA only reliably available for Yash and Apar across sources; treat the EV/EBITDA column as sparse.

**Closest comparables.** Yash is a **niche bushings specialist**, not a full transformer OEM, so no listed peer is a clean match. In descending order of relevance:
- **Shilchar Technologies** — best proxy: similar-size Gujarat-based niche transformer/components maker, debt-light, export-leveraged, high-ROCE. Trades at **~29x P/E vs Yash's ~54x** despite *higher* ROCE (50.7% vs 28.7%) and comparable margins.
- **Transformers & Rectifiers (TARIL)** and **Voltamp** — mid-cap transformer OEMs, the most natural T&D comps, at **32–36x P/E**.
- **HBL Engineering** — high-growth (67% TTM), high-ROCE adjacent capital-goods name at only **26x**.
- **Hitachi Energy India, GE Vernova T&D, CG Power** — large-cap T&D bellwethers; useful for sentiment but mismatched on scale, liquidity and float. Their extreme multiples (99x–149x) reflect mega-cap T&D euphoria and very high incremental ROCE, not a like-for-like read for an SME.
- **Apar** — conductor/oils play, only loosely comparable.

## 2. Where Yash's ~54x sits, and PEG

On a **trailing P/E basis Yash is the most expensive name in the cohort except the three large-cap T&D giants** (Hitachi 149x, CG 117x, GVT&D 99x). Against the genuinely comparable smaller specialists it carries a **large premium**:
- vs Shilchar ~29x → **~1.8x** the multiple, despite Shilchar's much higher ROCE.
- vs TARIL/Voltamp ~32–36x → **~50–70% premium**.
- vs HBL ~26x → roughly **2x**, even though HBL grew faster on TTM and earns far higher ROCE.

**PEG (rough):** On ~54x P/E against ~50–58% trailing earnings CAGR, PEG ≈ **0.9–1.1** — not obviously stretched *if* that growth rate persists. But against management's own *forward* guidance of ~35–40% CAGR, PEG rises to **~1.4–1.5**, i.e. demanding. Shilchar at ~29x on 3-yr 33% revenue / 54% profit growth screens at a *lower* PEG with *better* returns, which is the crux of the relative-value gap.

**Is the premium justified by growth/margins?** Partly. Yash's ~24% OPM and ~29% ROCE are solid, and bushings are a higher-barrier niche than commodity transformers (a cited "structural global shortage of HV/RIP bushings" supports pricing power). But its ROCE is *below* Shilchar, GVT&D and HBL, and its margins are comparable-to-Shilchar rather than superior. So the premium rests mainly on (a) faster recent top-line growth and (b) scarcity/SME-momentum, not on best-in-class returns. On fundamentals alone, the multiple looks **richer than the return profile strictly warrants relative to Shilchar/HBL**.

## 3. Growth implied by the multiple

At ~54x trailing on a ~24% margin, single-digit-billion-rupee base, the market is **extrapolating several more years of 40%+ growth**, broadly in line with the bull case of ₹600–700 Cr revenue within 2–3 years (from ₹235 Cr FY26) plus margin hold. Crude reverse-DCF intuition: a ~54x trailing multiple at a ~12–13% cost of equity only "makes sense" if earnings roughly **triple over ~3–4 years and then fade to mid-teens growth** — i.e. the price embeds the greenfield (Savli) ramp executing on time and exports scaling. It is **pricing in continued ~40–50% near-term growth**, not a normalized 20%. EV/EBITDA ~30x tells the same story and sits above Apar's ~21x.

## 4. Post-listing multiple history (de-rate vs re-rate)

Yash listed on **BSE SME on ~19 Dec 2024** at ₹146 (IPO P/E ~27x on FY24 EPS). The path since:
- Early 2025: brief de-rate, 52-wk low **₹150 (18 Mar 2025)** — essentially flat to IPO.

The stock is *not* de-rated; it sits near the top of its post-listing valuation range and Trendlyne flags an "Expensive Valuation" score; MoneyWorks4Me/ValueResearch flag it trading above its 3-yr historic valuation band.

## 5. Brokerage / analyst targets

## Synthesis — demanding, fair, or cheap?

On a **relative basis, the valuation is demanding.** Against its truest comparables — Shilchar (~29x, higher ROCE), TARIL/Voltamp (~32–36x), HBL (~26x, faster TTM growth, far higher ROCE) — Yash's ~54x P/E and ~11x P/B is a **substantial premium not fully explained by superior returns**; its ROCE/OPM are good but not best-in-class. It only looks "mid-pack" when benchmarked against large-cap T&D names (Hitachi 149x, CG 117x, GVT&D 99x), which are poor like-for-like comps for an SME.

On a **growth-adjusted basis the read is conditional.** Using trailing ~50%+ growth, PEG ~0.9–1.1 is reasonable; using management's own forward ~35–40% guidance, PEG ~1.4–1.5 is full; and on a normalized 20–25%, PEG ~2.2–2.7 is expensive. **Bottom line (no recommendation):** The stock has re-rated, not de-rated, since its Dec-2024 listing and now trades near the top of its history and at a clear premium to its closest small-cap peers. The premium is **justified only if elevated growth and margins continue**; it is **not justified by current returns relative to Shilchar/HBL**, and it carries **high sensitivity to any growth normalization** (~35–45% potential multiple compression toward peer norms). No credible analyst fair-value exists to triangulate against — a notable information gap for a name at this valuation.

---

# Yash Highvoltage Ltd (BSE SME 544310) — Valuation Read

**Framing:** This is a relative- and growth-adjusted valuation read on Yash Highvoltage, a ~₹2,100 Cr high-voltage transformer-bushing maker trading at ~54x P/E and ~11x P/B as of mid-June 2026. I assess where its multiple sits versus T&D-equipment peers, whether the premium is justified by growth/margins, what growth the multiple implies, the post-listing re-rating, available analyst views, and sensitivity to growth normalization. Findings only — no buy/sell.

**Important data caveats up front:**
- Yash is a **BSE SME** stock. Sell-side broker coverage is essentially absent — Trendlyne shows "No estimates available," and no mainstream brokerage price target exists. The only "targets" I found are from low-quality forecasting blogs (moneymintidea), which I do not treat as credible.
- Yash's own data has a **reported inconsistency**: Tickertape shows an FY24 EPS of ₹40.09 then FY25 EPS of ₹7.50 — that jump reflects a bonus/split or face-value change (FV is ₹5), not a real collapse. Treat per-share history pre-listing with caution; absolute revenue/profit figures are reliable.
- Figures are pulled across Screener, Tickertape, Trendlyne and Groww between ~9–13 June 2026; small differences across platforms reflect timing and TTM-vs-FY conventions. I flag these where they matter.

## Yash baseline (Screener/Tickertape, ~12 June 2026)
- Price ~₹735; market cap ~₹2,102 Cr; **P/E ~54** (Screener) to ~56 (Tickertape); **P/B ~11.4x** (Screener); EV/EBITDA ~30x (per ValueResearch/MarketsMojo data).
- FY26 sales ₹235 Cr, net profit ₹37 Cr, OPM ~24–25%, ROCE ~28.7%, ROE ~23.5%.
- Growth: FY24 ₹108 Cr → FY25 ₹150 Cr → FY26 ₹235 Cr revenue (~57% TTM sales growth, ~44% 5-yr CAGR). H1 FY26 revenue reportedly +78.6% YoY.
- Order book ~₹300–400 Cr; management guidance of ~35–40% CAGR over 5 years (and an aspirational "8–10x in 10 years"); Savli greenfield plant commercial production targeted H2 FY27.

## 1. Peer comparison table

All figures sourced from Screener.in company pages (consolidated where applicable), data dated ~2–12 June 2026. "Closest comparables" judgment follows the table.

| Company | Mkt cap (₹ Cr) | P/E | P/B | EV/EBITDA | Rev growth (TTM) | ROCE | OPM (FY26) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Yash Highvoltage** | ~2,102 | **~54** | **~11.4** | ~30 | ~57% | 28.7% | ~24% |
| Shilchar Technologies | ~4,616 | 29.2 | 9.4 | n/a | ~5% (3-yr 33%) | 50.7% | 29% |
| Voltamp Transformers | ~9,833 | 32.2 | ~5.5* | n/a | ~mid-teens | 24% | 16% |
| Transformers & Rectifiers (TARIL) | ~9,458 | 35.8 | 6.2 | n/a | 24% | 23.3% | 15% |
| Hitachi Energy India (POWERINDIA) | ~1,52,995 | 149 | 29.6 | n/a | 28% | 29.0% | 15% |
| GE Vernova T&D India (GVT&D) | ~1,26,553 | ~99 | 47.0 | n/a | 45% | 76.4% | 27% |
| CG Power & Industrial | ~1,44,022 | 117 | 18.1 | n/a | 25% | 27.0% | 13% |
| Apar Industries | ~61,149 | 61.1 | 11.3 | ~21 | 23% | 31.1% | 8% |
| HBL Engineering (ex-HBL Power) | ~21,463 | 26.2 | 9.9 | n/a | 67% | 59.4% | 34% |

\*Voltamp P/B computed from price ₹9,720 / book ₹1,771 ≈ 5.5x. EV/EBITDA only reliably available for Yash and Apar across sources; treat the EV/EBITDA column as sparse.

**Closest comparables.** Yash is a **niche bushings specialist**, not a full transformer OEM, so no listed peer is a clean match. In descending order of relevance:
- **Shilchar Technologies** — best proxy: similar-size Gujarat-based niche transformer/components maker, debt-light, export-leveraged, high-ROCE. Trades at **~29x P/E vs Yash's ~54x** despite *higher* ROCE (50.7% vs 28.7%) and comparable margins.
- **Transformers & Rectifiers (TARIL)** and **Voltamp** — mid-cap transformer OEMs, the most natural T&D comps, at **32–36x P/E**.
- **HBL Engineering** — high-growth (67% TTM), high-ROCE adjacent capital-goods name at only **26x**.
- **Hitachi Energy India, GE Vernova T&D, CG Power** — large-cap T&D bellwethers; useful for sentiment but mismatched on scale, liquidity and float. Their extreme multiples (99x–149x) reflect mega-cap T&D euphoria and very high incremental ROCE, not a like-for-like read for an SME.
- **Apar** — conductor/oils play, only loosely comparable.

## 2. Where Yash's ~54x sits, and PEG

On a **trailing P/E basis Yash is the most expensive name in the cohort except the three large-cap T&D giants** (Hitachi 149x, CG 117x, GVT&D 99x). Against the genuinely comparable smaller specialists it carries a **large premium**:
- vs Shilchar ~29x → **~1.8x** the multiple, despite Shilchar's much higher ROCE.
- vs TARIL/Voltamp ~32–36x → **~50–70% premium**.
- vs HBL ~26x → roughly **2x**, even though HBL grew faster on TTM and earns far higher ROCE.

**PEG (rough):** On ~54x P/E against ~50–58% trailing earnings CAGR, PEG ≈ **0.9–1.1** — not obviously stretched *if* that growth rate persists. But against management's own *forward* guidance of ~35–40% CAGR, PEG rises to **~1.4–1.5**, i.e. demanding. Shilchar at ~29x on 3-yr 33% revenue / 54% profit growth screens at a *lower* PEG with *better* returns, which is the crux of the relative-value gap.

**Is the premium justified by growth/margins?** Partly. Yash's ~24% OPM and ~29% ROCE are solid, and bushings are a higher-barrier niche than commodity transformers (a cited "structural global shortage of HV/RIP bushings" supports pricing power). But its ROCE is *below* Shilchar, GVT&D and HBL, and its margins are comparable-to-Shilchar rather than superior. So the premium rests mainly on (a) faster recent top-line growth and (b) scarcity/SME-momentum, not on best-in-class returns. On fundamentals alone, the multiple looks **richer than the return profile strictly warrants relative to Shilchar/HBL**.

## 3. Growth implied by the multiple

At ~54x trailing on a ~24% margin, single-digit-billion-rupee base, the market is **extrapolating several more years of 40%+ growth**, broadly in line with the bull case of ₹600–700 Cr revenue within 2–3 years (from ₹235 Cr FY26) plus margin hold. Crude reverse-DCF intuition: a ~54x trailing multiple at a ~12–13% cost of equity only "makes sense" if earnings roughly **triple over ~3–4 years and then fade to mid-teens growth** — i.e. the price embeds the greenfield (Savli) ramp executing on time and exports scaling. It is **pricing in continued ~40–50% near-term growth**, not a normalized 20%. EV/EBITDA ~30x tells the same story and sits above Apar's ~21x.

## 4. Post-listing multiple history (de-rate vs re-rate)

Yash listed on **BSE SME on ~19 Dec 2024** at ₹146 (IPO P/E ~27x on FY24 EPS). The path since:
- Early 2025: brief de-rate, 52-wk low **₹150 (18 Mar 2025)** — essentially flat to IPO.
- Mid-2025 onward: sharp **re-rate**, 52-wk high **₹873**; P/E expanded to ~54–58x by Sep 2025–May 2026 (third-party trackers: 54.4x Sep-2025, 57.8x Mar-2026, 58.1x May-2026).

So the dominant move since listing is a **re-rating from ~27x to ~54–58x — roughly a doubling of the multiple** — driven by the H1 FY26 ~78% revenue surge and the bushing-shortage narrative. The stock is *not* de-rated; it sits near the top of its post-listing valuation range and Trendlyne flags an "Expensive Valuation" score; MoneyWorks4Me/ValueResearch flag it trading above its 3-yr historic valuation band.

## 5. Brokerage / analyst targets

**None of institutional quality.** Trendlyne: "No estimates available." No mainstream brokerage (Motilal, Kotak, ICICI, etc.) publishes a price target — consistent with SME-platform status and thin float. The only numeric "targets" surfaced are from non-credible forecasting blogs and are not usable. **Confidence that no reliable analyst fair-value exists: high.**

## 6. Sensitivity if growth normalizes to ~20–25%

This is the central risk to the thesis. Illustrative, not a forecast:
- A ~54x P/E on a business compounding ~20–25% implies **PEG ~2.2–2.7** — clearly demanding by conventional standards. High-quality 20–25% growers in this space (Voltamp, TARIL) trade at **30–36x**.
- If the market re-rated Yash toward a ~30–35x "normal-grower" multiple while earnings still grew, the **multiple compression alone is ~35–45%** — a meaningful headwind that strong earnings would have to outrun.
- The asymmetry: at this multiple the price is underwritten by execution of the Savli greenfield ramp and export scaling. Any slip to 20–25% growth, or margin give-back as the bushing shortage eases, would likely trigger de-rating, since there is little valuation cushion (P/B ~11x leaves no asset-value support).
- Conversely, if 40–50% growth *persists* for 2–3 more years, today's 54x compresses rapidly on forward earnings and the entry looks far less stretched — which is precisely what bulls are paying for.

## Synthesis — demanding, fair, or cheap?

On a **relative basis, the valuation is demanding.** Against its truest comparables — Shilchar (~29x, higher ROCE), TARIL/Voltamp (~32–36x), HBL (~26x, faster TTM growth, far higher ROCE) — Yash's ~54x P/E and ~11x P/B is a **substantial premium not fully explained by superior returns**; its ROCE/OPM are good but not best-in-class. It only looks "mid-pack" when benchmarked against large-cap T&D names (Hitachi 149x, CG 117x, GVT&D 99x), which are poor like-for-like comps for an SME.

On a **growth-adjusted basis the read is conditional.** Using trailing ~50%+ growth, PEG ~0.9–1.1 is reasonable; using management's own forward ~35–40% guidance, PEG ~1.4–1.5 is full; and on a normalized 20–25%, PEG ~2.2–2.7 is expensive. The multiple is effectively a **bet that 40%+ growth and ~24% margins persist through the greenfield/export ramp.** That is plausible given a cited structural HV-bushing shortage and a ~₹300–400 Cr order book, but it is an execution-dependent bet with little margin of safety.

**Bottom line (no recommendation):** The stock has re-rated, not de-rated, since its Dec-2024 listing and now trades near the top of its history and at a clear premium to its closest small-cap peers. The premium is **justified only if elevated growth and margins continue**; it is **not justified by current returns relative to Shilchar/HBL**, and it carries **high sensitivity to any growth normalization** (~35–45% potential multiple compression toward peer norms). No credible analyst fair-value exists to triangulate against — a notable information gap for a name at this valuation.

I now have consistent, well-corroborated data. I have everything needed to report. Here is my synthesis.

---

# Yash Highvoltage Limited (BSE SME: 544310; CIN U40109GJ2002PLC040833) — Verification Report

**Important sourcing caveat up front:** I downloaded the official DRHP (412 pages, hosted on BSE) but this environment has no PDF text-extraction/rendering capability, so I could **not** read the prospectus internals directly. Items that live only inside the prospectus (the exact customer table, formal ageing schedules, the litigation chapter) are therefore corroborated via secondary sources that quote the company, and I flag each as such. Most-load-bearing numbers (shareholding, FY26 results, cash flow) come from filings mirrored on Screener and the company's own results coverage, cross-checked across two or more sources.

A second hazard worth noting: there is a sister/affiliate company, **Yash Optics & Lens Ltd** (NSE Emerge), which is easy to confuse with this company. I have kept them separate; one search inadvertently surfaced the Optics DRHP on NSE — that is **not** this company.

---

## 1. Listing venue — CONFIRMED: BSE SME (not NSE Emerge)

The shares are listed on the **BSE SME platform**, listed 19 Dec 2024. This is confirmed across multiple independent sources, including a primary-grade outlet: Business Standard's headline literally reads "**BSE SME** Yash Highvoltage's IPO ends with 169 times subscription." Screener and Groww both show exchange = BSE, code 544310, SME segment. The IPO was a book-built SME issue of 75,35,000 shares at ₹146 (face value ₹5), aggregating ~₹110.01 crore, bidding 12–16 Dec 2024.

**Verdict:** Sources claiming NSE Emerge are wrong (likely conflating it with Yash Optics & Lens). It is **BSE SME**. High confidence.

## 2. Promoter shareholding and pledging

- **Promoter holding: 57.94% as of the most recent quarter (Mar 2026)**, essentially flat over the year (Mar 2025: 57.89% → Sep 2025: 57.94% → Mar 2026: 57.94%). Source: Screener (mirroring BSE shareholding filings).
- **Pledging: No promoter pledge is disclosed** in the shareholding pattern on Screener or Groww. I found no filing indicating any pledged promoter shares.
- **Promoter:** Mr. Keyur Girishchandra Shah (corroborated across Chittorgarh-class sources and LinkedIn).

**Verdict:** ~57.94%, no disclosed pledge. Confidence high on the percentage; "no pledge" is high-confidence-but-by-absence (I could not open the BSE shareholding PDF itself to confirm a literal 0.00% pledge line).

## 3. The ~₹150 crore fundraise — approved as a ceiling, instrument not yet fixed

- The board met **13 May 2026** to consider FY26 results and "fund raising of up to ₹150 crore."
- **Shareholder approval for up to ₹150 crore has been obtained**, but management indicated it may not use the full limit — it is **targeting ~₹100–110 crore** (per the FY26 earnings-call coverage, multibagg.ai).
- **Instrument (QIP / preferential / rights) is NOT yet specified/finalised** in any source I could reach — the resolutions are framed as an enabling ceiling.
- **Intended use:** primarily **capex** — the greenfield/expansion RIP-bushing facility at Vadodara, extending product range up to **550 kV** and strengthening testing infrastructure. Some early coverage also framed it loosely as "growth and debt repayment," but the more detailed earnings-call coverage emphasises capex; note the company runs low leverage (debt/equity ~0.17x), so debt repayment is a minor element at most.

**Verdict:** Approved as a ceiling, not a committed/closed raise; instrument undetermined. I could NOT verify from a primary filing (postal-ballot/EGM result PDF) the exact resolution wording or whether it specifies QIP vs preferential — flag this as unverified at the primary level.

## 4. FY26 audited cash flow vs profit — CFO well below PAT

- **Net profit (PAT), FY26 (consolidated, YE 31 Mar 2026): ₹37.34 crore** (₹374 million), up ~75% YoY. Revenue ₹235.16 cr (+57%), EBITDA ₹60.4 cr (margin ~25.7%), EPS ₹13.08.
- **Cash flow from operations (CFO): ₹8.82 crore** (stated as "₹882.02 Lakhs," corroborated independently by Screener's "~₹9 cr" operating cash flow line).
- **CFO/PAT ≈ 0.24 (roughly 24%).** Operating cash conversion is weak — CFO is far below reported profit.
- Screener also shows FY26 **investing cash flow ≈ −₹15 cr** and **financing ≈ +₹1 cr**.

**Why the gap (corroborating evidence):** working capital ballooned with the 57% revenue surge — inventory roughly doubled and receivables rose (see point 5). This is the proximate cause of the low CFO/PAT.

**Verdict:** CFO ≈ ₹8.8 cr vs PAT ₹37.3 cr — a material divergence. Confidence high (two independent sources agree on ~₹9 cr / ₹882 lakh).

## 5. Receivables and inventory — sharp working-capital build; formal ageing NOT verified

- **Inventory: ₹66.65 crore (FY26) vs ₹29.67 crore (FY25)** — more than doubled.
- **Trade receivables: ₹30.57 crore (FY26) vs ₹27.73 crore (FY25)** — modest rise.
- The inventory surge is consistent with the order book climbing to ~₹400 cr (from ₹150 cr a year earlier) and the expansion build-out, and explains most of the CFO shortfall.

**Verdict:** I could NOT obtain the formal **ageing schedules** (the bucketed <6m / 6m–1y / >1y tables from the notes/annual report) from any primary source I could open. Only the headline balances are verified. Flag the ageing breakdown as **unverified**.

## 6. Customer list — partially corroborated; aggregator claims are a mix of accurate and unverified

What I can support from company-attributable sources:
- **Global MNC OEMs:** The company (via a transformer-technology.com profile quoting it) names "**Siemens, GE, Hitachi-ABB, Toshiba, CG, Schneider, Hyundai, BTW, and many more**." This supports the aggregator claims of **Hitachi and GE** (and Siemens), framed as OEM customers.
- **Indian utilities:** The FY26/FY25 directors' report references "premier central Utilities such as **POWERGRID & NTPC**," and notes its dry-type RIP bushings are embedded in POWERGRID/NTPC technical specifications and the CEA standard spec. This supports **NTPC and POWERGRID** — but as utilities whose specs drive demand / as approved-vendor relationships, not necessarily as direct purchasers.
- A company blog lists older domestic customers (Voltamp Transformers Vadodara, Crompton Greaves, EMCO, AREVA, MSEB, NEEPCO, Vijai Electricals, etc.) — this supports **Voltamp**, but the blog is dated (2009-era) and not the prospectus.
- **TARIL (Transformers & Rectifiers India) and Atlanta Electricals:** I found **no company-attributable confirmation** that these are customers. In the sources I read they appear only as **peer/comparable companies** in the transformer space, not as named Yash customers. The aggregator's pairing of them with Yash's customer list looks like a likely conflation.

**Verdict:**
- Supported as customers/partners: Siemens, GE, Hitachi(-ABB), Toshiba, CG, Schneider, Hyundai; and NTPC/POWERGRID (utility/spec relationships); Voltamp (historical).
- **NOT verified as customers: TARIL and Atlanta Electricals** — treat the aggregator claim with skepticism; they read as peers, not clients.
- The **exact prospectus customer table** I could not read (DRHP unreadable here), so I cannot confirm precisely which names the RHP itself lists as material customers. Flag as partially unverified.

## 7. SEBI actions, auditor qualifications, material litigation — none found

- **Auditor opinion FY26: UNMODIFIED (clean).** Per BSE-filing coverage on Screener: "Auditor issued unmodified opinion for FY26 results" (17 May 2026). The FY25 directors' report similarly records the statutory auditor's report with no reservations and the secretarial audit "without any qualification, reservation, adverse remark or disclaimer."
- **Material litigation / regulatory orders: none disclosed.** FY25 directors' report states "no significant and material order was passed by the Regulators or courts."
- **SEBI actions against company/promoter:** I found **no** SEBI order, investigation, or enforcement action against Yash Highvoltage or Mr. Keyur Shah in any source searched.
- Note (not adverse): the company completed a **₹5.24 cr acquisition of a 50% stake in Sukrut Electric Company** (drives the consolidated FY26 reporting). This is corporate activity, not litigation.

**Verdict:** No auditor qualifications, no material litigation, no SEBI action found. Confidence moderate-to-high — this is partly an "absence of evidence" finding; I could not open the prospectus litigation chapter or SEBI's order database directly, so I cannot rule out minor/ordinary-course matters disclosed inside the DRHP.
