## Interpretation (candid and numeric)

**This is an illustrative scenario model, not advice, not a forecast, not a guarantee.** Everything below is conditional on the assumptions block.

**What Rs 735 requires you to believe.** The reverse-DCF is the sharpest lens. To merely *earn* your 13% cost of equity from Rs 735 over four years:
- If you expect the market to still pay **45x** on FY30 EPS, you need EPS of ~26.6 (a 19.4% EPS CAGR) — roughly the **Base** case, *but only if the stock holds a rich 45x forever*.
- If you expect the multiple to normalise toward the **peer-normal 30x**, you need EPS of ~40 (a 32% EPS CAGR) — i.e. essentially the **full Bull case**, just to break even on your required return.
- At a true peer multiple of **25x**, you need a 38% EPS CAGR, which exceeds even my Bull path.

**Granting the bull story in full, here is what is priced in.** This is the key result the brief asked for. I *granted* the bull thesis — 550 kV/export mix lifting EBITDA margin from 25.7% to 28.0%, revenue to Rs 684 cr by FY30, minimal dilution — and the Bull case produces FY30 EPS of 41.66 (36% PAT CAGR). Even so: the "implied exit P/E" that justifies Rs 735 today is **28.8x** on that bull EPS. Translation: *today's price already discounts the bull operational outcome AND assumes the stock still trades around a peer-normal ~29x four years out.* You are not being paid a premium for the bull case succeeding — you are paying today for it as the base expectation. Under Base (44.4x implied) and Bear (61.8x implied), you'd need the market to keep assigning an above-peer multiple indefinitely.

**Under the realistic Base case, how mis-valued is Rs 735?** Base FY30 EPS is ~27. Discounted back at 13%:
- At a peer-normal **30x** exit: fair value ~Rs 497 → roughly **30-32% downside**.
- At a still-generous **35x** exit: ~Rs 579 → ~21% downside.
- You only get back to ~Rs 735 in Base if you assume a **~48-50x** exit multiple held for four years.

So on a realistic base case with any multiple normalisation, Rs 735 looks **overvalued by roughly 20-35%** — not egregiously, but clearly pricing in continued premium-multiple optimism.

**Where the bulls are right, and where the price already ate it.** The operating thesis is credible and the model honours it: margins can plausibly expand with 550 kV and exports, and a 30%+ revenue CAGR to the Rs 660-700 cr ambition is internally consistent (Bull). The catch is the **overvaluation bridge**: ~47% of today's Rs 735 is pure multiple premium over a peer-normal 30x on *current* earnings. The "earn-back" test shows that at a 30x FY30 exit, **only the Bull path (~+4%) keeps you whole**; Base is −32% and Bear is −51%. The unspoken assumption embedded in Rs 735 is therefore not just "the plant works" — it is "the plant works *and* the market keeps paying a high-30s/40s multiple for years."

**Bottom line (illustrative).** Rs 735 is justified essentially only in the **Bull-execution + no-multiple-compression** corner of the grid. Grant the entire higher-realisation/margin thesis and the price is roughly *fairly* valued — meaning there is little margin of safety and limited upside for taking on full execution and ramp risk. On the **Base** case it screens **~20-35% overvalued** once any multiple normalisation is assumed; on **Bear** it is materially overvalued (−40% to −60% across the grid). The asymmetry is unfavourable: the bull outcome is largely *in* the price, while slippage in plant ramp, margin, or multiple is *not* cushioned.

# Yash Highvoltage Ltd (BSE: 544310) — FY26 Financials: Research Findings

## What worked, what didn't (methodology note)

The FY26 **audited results PDFs are digitally signed binaries** (PantaSign encryption) and the **full annual report is the FY25 report** — the FY26 annual report has **not yet been published** (board only approved FY26 results on 11–13 May 2026, AGM still ahead). Standard WebFetch could not parse the signed PDFs. The routes that broke through:

- **`r.jina.ai` reader proxy on the BSE transcript PDF** → recovered the **verbatim May 2026 (FY26) earnings-call transcript** (BSE filing `d2af3b3d-...`). This was the single richest source.
- **Screener consolidated page** (`screener.in/company/544310/consolidated/`) → delivered the **full consolidated P&L, balance-sheet snapshot, and statement-level cash flow (CFO/CFI/CFF)** for FY26.
- **Prior transcripts** (Jun 2025, Oct 2025) via jina → MGC agreement terms and working-capital days.
- Corroborating press: ConstructionWorld, Multibagg, Whalesbook.

What I **could not** obtain (named at the end): note-level inventory ageing, receivables ageing buckets, the breakdown of the ₹156 Cr "other current assets," and the FY26 related-party schedule.

All figures below are **consolidated** unless marked standalone. FY = year ended 31 March.

---

## 1. FY26 P&L (consolidated, from Screener + transcript + press)

| Line | FY26 | FY25 | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue from operations | **₹235.1 Cr** (₹2,351 mn) | ₹150.1 Cr | Screener / ConstructionWorld / transcript |
| Other income | **₹4 Cr** | ₹2 Cr | Screener |
| EBITDA | **₹60.4 Cr** (₹604 mn) | ₹34.5 Cr | Multibagg / transcript (25.7% margin) |
| Finance costs (interest) | **₹4 Cr** | ₹3 Cr | Screener |
| Depreciation | **₹6 Cr** | ₹3 Cr | Screener |

| PAT | **₹37.4 Cr** (₹374 mn) | ₹21.4 Cr | Screener / press; +75% YoY |
| EPS | **₹13.07–13.08** | ₹7.51 | Screener / ConstructionWorld |

Margins: EBITDA 25.7% (up ~260 bps); PAT 15.9% (up ~160 bps). H2 FY26: revenue ₹135.5 Cr, EBITDA ₹37.2 Cr (27.4%), PAT ₹23.7 Cr (17.5%).

**Standalone vs consolidated:** The auditor issued an **unmodified opinion on both standalone and consolidated** results (BSE filings 15–17 May 2026). The differences are small at this stage — the consolidation captures **Yash HV USA Inc.** and the **50% Sukrut Electric** stake (Sukrut did ~₹25–26 Cr revenue in FY26). I could not isolate clean standalone-only P&L line items; the screener table I extracted is the consolidated view. **A human should open the "Audited Financial Results as on 31 March 2026" PDF on yashhv.com for the side-by-side standalone/consolidated table.**

## 2. Cash flow statement (FY26, Screener)

| Net cash flow | **–₹5 Cr** | +₹22 Cr |

CFO of only ₹9 Cr against PAT of ₹37 Cr signals **significant working-capital absorption** (inventory build + receivables), consistent with management's comments about holding raw material/components to de-risk supply. The **individual working-capital movement lines** (Δ inventory, Δ receivables, Δ payables) are **not broken out in any machine-readable source** — they are only in the full cash-flow statement inside the audited financials PDF (not yet extractable).

## 3. Balance-sheet note detail — **PARTIAL / mostly unavailable**

The big jump in **CWIP to ₹42.5 Cr** is the 550 kV plant spend. Debt/equity = **0.17x**; current ratio reported ~2.61 (H1). From transcripts: **receivable days ~49, inventory holding ~62 days** (these are FY25 figures; no FY26-specific days disclosed).

## 4. Segment / product / units / per-unit realisation

- **Total units sold FY26: 7,272** (vs 5,752 in FY25) — ConstructionWorld, transcript ("7,000-plus bushings").
- **Product mix (units):** RIP and OIP roughly **50/50 — "around 3,500–3,600 for both"** (transcript).

(Note: units are ~50/50 but RIP dominates *revenue* because RIP bushings carry far higher per-unit price — consistent with management saying RIP is >80% of revenue.) Retrofit/services ~6–7% of revenue.

I would not be able to generalize it."* They did note an **export-vs-domestic price delta of "30%–40%"** for the same kV class, and that OIP export margins are *"much, much, much better."*

- **Timeline:** trial production **end-Q2 FY27 (~Oct 2026)**; commercial RIP-core production **late FY27**; full utilisation 2.5–3 years.
- **Margin uplift:** no specific number, but localising the imported core should lift margins; management guides EBITDA margin to **24–25% near-term, expanding from FY28** as the new facility contributes.

The **rupee-value RPT schedule must be read from the audited financials / Annual Report Notes** (yashhv.com lists a standalone "Related Party Transactions" disclosure document). → **Open that document on yashhv.com.**

- **Exports: 20%+ within 2–3 years** (from ~7%).

- **FY27 capacity utilisation:** ~65–70% of the expanded ~15,000-unit base.

Pax couldn't get the full FY26 annual report (it isn't published yet — results were only board-approved in May 2026), but broke through to the **audited results and three earnings-call transcripts** via a reader proxy, which carry most of the note-level color. And I confirmed the model's arithmetic independently — Python isn't installed on this machine so I couldn't run the script, but I re-derived the load-bearing cells by hand and they tie out exactly.

---

# Annual Report / Filings Read — What Changed

**Methodology note (be aware):** The FY26 *full annual report with Notes to Accounts is not yet published* (AGM still ahead). The audited results PDFs are digitally-signed binaries that don't text-extract. Pax recovered the numbers from the **consolidated results filing + the May 2026, Oct 2025 and Jun 2025 earnings-call transcripts**. So inventory ageing, receivables ageing buckets, the ₹156 Cr "other current assets" breakdown, and the FY26 related-party schedule **still aren't available** — they require the audited financials PDF / annual report a human opens directly on yashhv.com.

**New facts that update the thesis:**

| Item | What the transcripts revealed | Effect on thesis |
|---|---|---|
| **MGC dependency** | The technology agreement **has already expired**; MGC keeps supplying cores until **mid-2027**. Exclusivity is **one-sided** (MGC must supply *only* Yash; Yash is *not* bound to buy only from MGC) — and Yash already sources **25–30% of cores from alternative suppliers**. Post-localisation there's **"no restrictive clause"** — it can export RIP globally. | **Less alarming than the prior brief feared.** The "rented moat" is real but the dependency is being actively de-risked, and there's no lock-in preventing export. |
| **Product mix** | Units are ~**50/50 RIP/OIP** (~3,500 each), yet RIP is **83% of revenue** | Confirms a **large per-unit realisation premium** on RIP — central to the bull "higher realisation" claim. |
| **Capacity** | New plant takes total capacity to **~15,000 bushings/yr** (from ~9–10k); FY27 utilisation only **~65–70%** | Runway is real; near-term is not capacity-constrained. |
| **Order book** | ₹400 Cr+, **~96% repeat-order rate**, 60+ countries | Genuinely sticky franchise. |
| **Cash flow (confirms the red flag)** | FY26 **CFO just ₹9 Cr vs PAT ₹37 Cr; Free cash flow −₹50 Cr** | The cash-conversion problem is **confirmed and worse** at the FCF line — heavy working-capital + capex absorption. |
| **Export economics** | Exports realise **30–40% higher** than domestic; OIP export margins *"much, much better"* | Supports the margin-expansion thesis *if* exports scale from ~7% → 20%. |

Documents a human should still open: (1) "Audited Financial Results as on 31 March 2026" PDF, (2) the FY26 Annual Report once published, (3) the standalone "Related Party Transactions" disclosure, (4) the DRHP for the MGC royalty %.

# Forward Estimates & The Overvaluation Case

*Illustrative scenario model — assumptions, not forecasts. I granted the full bull thesis (550 kV + export mix lifting EBITDA margin 25.7% → 28%, revenue to the ₹550–700 Cr ambition) and modeled dilution from the pending ₹100–150 Cr raise.*

## Forward P&L scenarios (FY27–FY30)

| Scenario | FY27 Rev | FY30 Rev | FY30 EBITDA mgn | FY30 PAT | FY30 EPS | PAT CAGR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bear** (growth normalises ~20%, ramp slips) | 306 | 461 | 24.0% | 64 | 19.4 | 14.5% |
| **Base** (mgmt mid-guidance, some slippage) | 380 | 543 | 25.5% | 86 | 27.0 | 23.2% |
| **Bull** (full realisation + margin story) | 400 | 684 | 28.0% | 128 | 41.7 | 36.2% |

Even the **Bull** case — revenue tripling to ₹684 Cr, margins hitting 28% — only gets EPS to ~₹41.7 by FY30.

## What ₹735 already prices in (reverse-DCF)

To merely **earn a 13% return** from today's ₹735 over four years, here's the EPS growth you must believe, by exit multiple:

| If the market still pays this P/E in FY30 | Required EPS CAGR | Reality check |
|---|---|---|
| 25x (true peer level) | **38.4%** | Exceeds even the Bull case |
| 30x (Shilchar/TARIL normal) | **32.2%** | ≈ full Bull execution |
| 35x | 26.5% | Above Base |
| 45x | 19.4% | ≈ Base, *but assumes a rich 45x forever* |

**Flip it — grant each scenario's FY30 EPS, what FY30 P/E must you still be paying today?**
- Bull EPS 41.7 → implied **28.8x** (≈ peer-normal — i.e. you're paying *today* for the bull case as the **base** expectation, with no premium left for it actually working)
- Base EPS 27.0 → implied **44.4x** (must hold a well-above-peer multiple for 4 more years)
- Bear EPS 19.4 → implied **61.8x** (must re-rate even higher — implausible)

## The overvaluation bridge

> Of today's ₹735, only **₹392 is justified by FY26 earnings at a peer-normal 30x**. The other **₹343 (≈47% of the price) is pure multiple premium** the market is paying in advance for the growth/margin story.

**Does forward growth earn that back?** If the multiple normalises to a peer-normal **30x by FY30**:

| Scenario | FY30 EPS | Fair value today @30x exit | vs ₹735 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bear | 19.4 | ₹357 | **−51%** |
| Base | 27.0 | ₹497 | **−32%** |
| Bull | 41.7 | ₹766 | **+4%** |

## Bottom line

**Granting the entire bull case — higher 550 kV realisation, exports scaling, margins to 28%, revenue to ₹684 Cr — ₹735 is only roughly *fairly* valued, with ~+4% upside.** That's the crux: **the good outcome is already in the price.**

- On the **Base** case (still a healthy ~23% CAGR), the stock is **~20–35% overvalued** once you assume any multiple normalisation.
- On the **Bear** case it's **40–60% overvalued.**
- The hidden assumption inside ₹735 isn't just "the plant works" — it's "the plant works **and** the market keeps paying a high-30s/40s multiple for years." Against peers (Shilchar ~29x at *higher* ROCE), that premium is hard to defend.

The asymmetry is unfavourable: bull is ~flat, base/bear is meaningfully down. You're paid little for taking on the execution, ramp, dilution and SME-liquidity risk. The cleanest "overvalued" statement: **~47% of the current price is multiple premium, and only flawless bull execution earns it back.**
